note: The video claims Mance said he was burned 'for all the world to see,' but this phrase does not appear in Mance's dialogue in the books. The Pink Letter says Mance is caged 'for all the north to see' (ADWD Jon XIII, line 233). The comparison is speculative.

The book shows 'Abel' (a singer) at Winterfell with six women, and the Pink Letter references 'the six whores who came with him to Winterfell' (referring to Mance). This is circumstantial evidence, but the book never explicitly confirms Abel's identity as Mance Rayder.

verbatim_quote: "Lord Stannis is outside the walls, and not far by the sound of it. All we need do is reach him." Abel's fingers danced across the strings of his lute. The singer's beard was brown, though his long hair had largely gone to grey. "If the Bastard does come after us, he might live long enough to rue it."
note: Abel (the singer) does speak with Theon and discusses escape to Stannis. The video theory that Abel = Mance is textually circumstantial but not explicitly confirmed by the books.

This beat asks 'why would Mance write the letter at all' - it is a rhetorical question/theoretical objection raised by the video, not a claim that can be grounded in book text. The Pink Letter's authorship remains ambiguous.
This beat characterizes a proposed plan as 'convoluted' - this is the video's theoretical judgment, not a factual claim in the book.
This beat asks why the letter demands 'Reek' - this is a theoretical objection raised by the video, not a claim that can be verified against book text.

verbatim_quote: He had come this close to telling them the truth when Rowan had delivered him to Abel in the ruins of the Burned Tower, but at the last instant he had held his tongue. The singer seemed intent on making off with the daughter of Eddard Stark. If he knew that Lord Ramsay's bride was but a steward's whelp, well …
note: This confirms Abel (the singer) has a plan at Winterfell, though the book does not explicitly state he investigates crypts. Later chapters show Barbrey Dustin investigates crypts, not Abel.

The video speculates that 'Abel' is an anagram of 'Bael' and connects this to Mance's potential interest in crypts and the Horn of Winter. The book never explicitly makes these connections or confirms Abel = Mance.
This beat is theoretical speculation about Stannis's motivations for potentially wanting wildling help - it cannot be grounded in book text.
This beat is theoretical speculation about why Stannis might write a fake letter instead of asking Jon directly - it cannot be grounded in book text.

This beat is theoretical speculation about Stannis's offer to make Jon Lord of Winterfell - while Stannis did make this offer in the books, the beat's claim that Jon 'might as well take up Stannis's offer' is speculative.

verbatim_quote: "Rather than use our swords upon each other, you might try them on Lord Stannis." Lord Bolton unrolled the parchment. "His host lies not three days' ride from here, snowbound and starving, and I for one am tired of waiting on his pleasure."
note: This confirms that Stannis is 'snowbound and starving.' The text also mentions Theon (who would become 'Reek') is at Winterfell, and Roose Bolton has strategic knowledge. However, the book does not explicitly confirm Stannis captured both a maester and Theon as the beat claims.

verbatim_quote: "His host lies not three days' ride from here, snowbound and starving, and I for one am tired of waiting on his pleasure. Ser Hosteen, assemble your knights and men-at-arms by the main gates."
note: Roose Bolton explicitly states Stannis's army is 'snowbound and starving,' confirming the beat's claim.
This beat is theoretical speculation about the timing of a hypothetical letter - the book text does not address this theory.

This beat is speculative characterization of Stannis as 'terrible with people and emotions' - while Stannis is described as having difficulty with emotions in the books, the beat's claim that forging a letter is 'not Stannis's style' is interpretive.

This beat's claim that the letter focuses too much on Mance is interpretive - the Pink Letter as written in the books does emphasize demands related to Mance and wildlings (the letter demands Val, the wildling babe, etc.), but the beat's characterization is theoretical.
This beat asks why Stannis would demand 'Reek' when he already has Theon - the book text does not provide an answer to this theoretical objection.

verbatim_quote: "Your sister." Melisandre put her hand on his arm. "You cannot help her, but he can."
note: This confirms Melisandre has schemes involving Mance (sent to help with 'Arya'). The text also shows she orchestrated Mance's glamor disguise and sent him on a mission to Winterfell.

verbatim_quote: "Look to the skies, Lord Snow. And when you have your answers, send to me. Winter is almost upon us now. I am your only hope."
note: Jon does suspect Melisandre has information or involvement with recent events (the Hardhome situation, the missing Arya). While not explicit about suspecting her role with Mance, the text shows Jon is uncertain about Melisandre's plans.

verbatim_quote: Snowflakes swirled from a dark sky and ashes rose to meet them, the grey and the white whirling around each other as flaming arrows arced above a wooden wall and dead things shambled silent through the cold, beneath a great grey cliff where fires burned inside a hundred caves. Then the wind rose and the white mist came sweeping in, impossibly cold, and one by one the fires went out. Afterward only the skulls remained.
note: This confirms Melisandre has visions in her fires that show possible future events. However, the beat's claim that she specifically predicts the Pink Letter's arrival is not explicitly stated in the book.

verbatim_quote: "Selyse has the right of this, Lord Snow. Let them die. You cannot save them. Your ships are lost—"
note: This shows Melisandre discusses the coming events with Jon and urges caution, but the specific claim that she 'warns Jon not to get stabbed' or 'believes Stannis is the chosen one' is partially supported but not the exact quote. The text shows her warning about Hardhome, not explicitly warning against getting stabbed.

verbatim_quote: It fell to Roger Ryswell and Barbrey Dustin to calm them with quiet words. Roose Bolton said nothing at all. But Theon Greyjoy saw a look in his pale eyes that he had never seen before—an uneasiness, even a hint of fear.
note: Barbrey Dustin is introduced as a mysterious figure who appears to be allied with the Boltons but seems to have other motives. The text hints at her secretive nature and political maneuvering.

verbatim_quote: The way was narrow and steep, the steps worn in the center by centuries of feet. They went single file—the serjeant with the lantern, then Theon and Lady Dustin, her other man behind them. He had always thought of the crypts as cold, and so they seemed in summer, but now as they descended the air grew warmer.
note: This confirms Barbrey Dustin takes Theon into the Winterfell crypts. The book does not show Mance/Abel asking about crypts, but shows Barbrey investigating them.

The beat claims Stannis will defeat the Freys using the 'beacon-and-lake trick' - the book never confirms this theory. While Stannis's army is at a frozen lake and he speaks of turning 'the ground' to his advantage, the book does not contain an explicit account of this battle plan succeeding or failing.
